tracking_notes: TROUBLESHOOTING, CITED_EVIDENCE, and EXTERNAL_STORY are tracked outside the optional-accent bucket.

chapters.thesis: Burnout is a specific condition with a specific signature — not tiredness, not stress, not laziness — and most people who have it are the last to name it correctly.
The real cost of burnout is not lost energy — it is lost caring. The exhaustion most people notice is the surface. What is disappearing underneath is the capacity to give a damn.

Underneath burnout is an equation the reader has never consciously examined: worth equals usefulness. Not worth is enhanced by usefulness — worth IS usefulness. When you stop producing, you stop mattering.

chapters.working_title: Your Body Was Telling the Truth
chapters.thesis: The physical collapse of burnout — the exhaustion that persists through sleep, the inflammation, the immune dysfunction — is not weakness. It is the body accurately reporting the cost of sustained overfunctioning. The body was never wrong.

chapters.working_title: Doing More to Feel Less Useless
chapters.thesis: Overfunctioning is not a personality type or a work ethic. It is a specific coping mechanism: when the threat of worthlessness rises, doing more temporarily suppresses it. The more has to keep increasing because the relief is temporary. This is the engine of burnout.

Rest is not the solution to burnout because burnout is not a rest deficit. Resting and returning to the same pattern will produce the same outcome. The reader's strategy is wrong — not inefficient, but wrong in kind.

chapters.working_title: Who Are You When You're Not Useful?
chapters.thesis: For many burnout sufferers, usefulness is not just a value — it is identity. The question "who am I when I'm not producing?" is not rhetorical. It is a genuine crisis of self-definition that the overfunctioning pattern has been deferring.

Overfunctioning doesn't stay at work. It moves into relationships as rescuing — doing for others what they could do for themselves, taking on emotional labor that isn't yours, making yourself indispensable. And the resentment that follows is not ingratitude; it is the cost of a system where no one is actually receiving care.

chapters.working_title: Capacity Is Not a Character Flaw
chapters.thesis: Human capacity is finite. This is not a weakness to overcome or a constraint to optimize around — it is a biological and psychological fact. Protecting finite capacity is not selfishness; it is accurate accounting.

The first practical move against overfunctioning is not adding rest — it is making one deliberate choice to not-do something you would have done. This is not the same as rest. It is a small act of defiance against the worth-equals-usefulness equation.

It is possible to have a different relationship to yourself — one in which your existence doesn't require continuous justification through productivity. This is not an insight that arrives intellectually. It is a relationship built through repeated small experiences of surviving the worthlessness dread.

Recovery from burnout is not a return to a previous state. The previous state produced the burnout. What is being built is something different: a self that has a sustainable relationship with its own capacity, built on worth that doesn't require depletion as proof.
